title: CDMGNotation
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG. We will write:

  1. $v \in G$ to mean $v \in J \cup V$,
  2. $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in E$,
  3. $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_2,v_1) \in E$,
  4. $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ to mean $(v_1,v_2) \in L$,
  5. $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  6. $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$,
  7. $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$ to mean that either $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G$ or $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$.

The star stands for a placeholder to mean: “arrowhead or tail”.

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_2 -- "CDMGNotation".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_2
% title: CDMGNotation
\def\rowref{def\_3\_2}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_2}

\begin{Not}[CDMGNotation]
    \label{not-cdmg}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}).
    We introduce the following seven shorthand notations, each defined as a literal abbreviation for a set-theoretic statement over the four components $J, V, E, L$ of $G$.
    The visual macros $\tuh, \hut, \huh, \suh, \hus, \sus$ are notation only; the load-bearing definitional content is the right-hand side of each abbreviation below.
    \begin{enumerate}
        %\item $G(V|\doit(J))$ to represent the graph $(J,V,E,L)$, where $E$ and $L$ are kept implicit in this notation.
        \item $v \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; v \in J \cup V$.
        \item $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in E$.
        \item $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_2, v_1) \in E$.
        \item $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in L$.
              Because $L$ is required to be symmetric by the CDMG definition (def \ref{def-cdmg}: $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Longleftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$ for all $v_1, v_2$), this abbreviation is itself symmetric in its two arguments:
              \[ v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \huh v_1 \in G. \]
        \item $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.
              Because the $\huh$-disjunct is symmetric in its arguments (item~4), swapping $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ in this disjunction turns $\suh$ into $\hus$:
              \[ v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \hus v_1 \in G. \]
        \item $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.
              By the same $\huh$-symmetry of item~4, $\hus$ becomes $\suh$ under swap of its arguments:
              \[ v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \suh v_1 \in G. \]
        \item $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.

              \emph{Exhaustiveness of the three disjuncts.}
              These three configurations enumerate every edge configuration a CDMG admits between an ordered pair of nodes $(v_1, v_2)$.
              Although mechanically reading the placeholder convention ``$\star$ stands for arrowhead or tail'' would suggest a fourth, tail-tail case ``$v_1 \tut v_2$'', no edge type of a CDMG carries tails on both ends: per def \ref{def-cdmg}, directed edges in $E$ have one tail and one head, and bidirected edges in $L$ have two heads, so no tail-tail edge type exists.
              The case $v_1 \tut v_2$ is therefore vacuous as a CDMG edge and is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight.
              Any case analysis on ``$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$'' may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

              \emph{Symmetry of $\sus$.}
              Under the swap $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$, the three disjuncts pair off: $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \hut v_1 \in G$ (items~2~$\leftrightarrow$~3), and $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$ by the $\huh$-symmetry of item~4. Hence $\sus$ is symmetric in its two arguments:
              \[ v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \sus v_1 \in G. \]
    \end{enumerate}
    A ``$\star$'' appearing in the diagrammatic shorthands ($\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$) is a placeholder reading ``arrowhead or tail''; in $\sus$ the would-be tail-tail combination is vacuous as a CDMG edge type (see item~7), so $\sus$ ranges over exactly the three configurations enumerated there.
\end{Not}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg). We introduce the following seven shorthand notations, each defined as a literal abbreviation for a set-theoretic statement over the four components $J, V, E, L$ of $G$. The visual macros $\tuh, \hut, \huh, \suh, \hus, \sus$ are notation only; the load-bearing definitional content is the right-hand side of each abbreviation below.

  1. $v \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; v \in J \cup V$.
  2. $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in E$.
  3. $v_1 \hut v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_2, v_1) \in E$.
  4. $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; (v_1, v_2) \in L$. Because $L$ is required to be symmetric by the CDMG definition (def def-cdmg: $(v_1, v_2) \in L \Longleftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$ for all $v_1, v_2$), this abbreviation is itself symmetric in its two arguments: \[ v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \huh v_1 \in G. \]
  5. $v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$. Because the $\huh$-disjunct is symmetric in its arguments (item 4), swapping $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ in this disjunction turns $\suh$ into $\hus$: \[ v_1 \suh v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \hus v_1 \in G. \]
  6. $v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$. By the same $\huh$-symmetry of item 4, $\hus$ becomes $\suh$ under swap of its arguments: \[ v_1 \hus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \suh v_1 \in G. \]
  7. $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;:\Longleftrightarrow\; \bigl(v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \hut v_2 \in G\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(v_1 \huh v_2 \in G\bigr)$, equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in E \,\lor\, (v_2, v_1) \in E \,\lor\, (v_1, v_2) \in L$.

    Exhaustiveness of the three disjuncts. These three configurations enumerate every edge configuration a CDMG admits between an ordered pair of nodes $(v_1, v_2)$. Although mechanically reading the placeholder convention “$\star$ stands for arrowhead or tail” would suggest a fourth, tail-tail case “$v_1 \tut v_2$”, no edge type of a CDMG carries tails on both ends: per def def-cdmg, directed edges in $E$ have one tail and one head, and bidirected edges in $L$ have two heads, so no tail-tail edge type exists. The case $v_1 \tut v_2$ is therefore vacuous as a CDMG edge and is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight. Any case analysis on “$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$” may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

    Symmetry of $\sus$. Under the swap $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$, the three disjuncts pair off: $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \hut v_1 \in G$ (items 2 $\leftrightarrow$ 3), and $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G$ exchanges with $v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$ by the $\huh$-symmetry of item 4. Hence $\sus$ is symmetric in its two arguments: \[ v_1 \sus v_2 \in G \;\Longleftrightarrow\; v_2 \sus v_1 \in G. \]

A “$\star$” appearing in the diagrammatic shorthands ($\suh$, $\hus$, $\sus$) is a placeholder reading “arrowhead or tail”; in $\sus$ the would-be tail-tail combination is vacuous as a CDMG edge type (see item 7), so $\sus$ ranges over exactly the three configurations enumerated there.

lean_blocks.explanation: Section-level `variable` declaration introducing the carrier type `Node` (implicit, universe-polymorphic via `Type*`) and a `DecidableEq Node` instance shared across every declaration that follows. Lean's `variable` mechanism auto-binds these arguments into any `def` or `instance` in the same scope that actually references them, so the seven main declarations need not repeat `{Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` individually. `DecidableEq Node` is the prerequisite for `Finset Node` membership to compute: `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` and the pair-membership clauses `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E` / `(v1, v2) ∈ G.L` all rely on `Finset`'s decidable membership, which itself needs decidable equality on the element type. Keeping the binder implicit (`{...}`) means call sites write `G.tuh v1 v2`, not `@G.tuh Node _ v1 v2`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide binders; auto-injected into following decls that use `Node`.
-- `Type*` is universe-polymorphic; `[DecidableEq Node]` powers `Finset` membership.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
instance instMembership : Membership Node (CDMG Node) where
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V
lean_blocks.explanation: Registers an instance of Mathlib's `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` typeclass so that the literal LN syntax `v ∈ G` elaborates on a CDMG without needing a custom function call like `G.Mem v`. The `Membership` typeclass dispatches the `∈` symbol based on the second type argument; here it reads `G : CDMG Node` on the right and selects this instance. The body `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` reuses `∈` a second time, but now against a `Finset Node`, so Lean transparently resolves the inner `∈` to `Finset.instMembership` and the outer to ours. The disjointness witness `hJV_disj` carried by `CDMG` is deliberately not threaded here: membership only asks whether `v` lies in `J` or `V`, not in exactly one. Downstream rows invoke `hJV_disj` directly when they need the exclusive form.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` lets `v ∈ G` parse verbatim on the CDMG.
-- The second type argument differs from `Finset Node`, so no clash with Mathlib's instance.
instance instMembership : Membership Node (CDMG Node) where
  -- `mem G v` is the predicate `∈` unfolds to; inner `∈` is `Finset` membership on the union.
  mem G v := v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V

lean_blocks.code: def tuh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.E
lean_blocks.explanation: Item 2 of the notation block: the primitive directed-edge relation. The name `tuh` encodes the LN glyph $\tuh$, read as tail-on-left, head-on-right; here that means the first argument `v1` is the source (tail) and the second `v2` is the target (head). The body is the literal `Finset` membership `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E`, mirroring the LN's $(v_1,v_2) \in E$. Returning `Prop` (rather than `Bool`) keeps the relation propositional and usable inside `∀ / ∃` quantifiers without `decide`. A plain `def` was preferred over `abbrev` so that downstream proofs choose when to `unfold CDMG.tuh`; dot-notation `G.tuh v1 v2` then reads left-to-right exactly like $v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `tuh` = tail-head: arrow from `v1` (tail) to `v2` (head); LN macro $\tuh$.
-- Body is literal `Finset (Node × Node)` membership; `Prop` keeps it usable in quantifiers.
def tuh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v1, v2) ∈ G.E

lean_blocks.code: def hut (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v2, v1) ∈ G.E
lean_blocks.explanation: Item 3: the backwards directed-edge relation. The letter pattern `hut` encodes head-on-left, tail-on-right — so `G.hut v1 v2` asserts the head sits at `v1` and the tail at `v2`. Since `G.E` stores ordered source-target pairs, this is `(v2, v1) ∈ G.E`: arrow from `v2` to `v1`. By construction `G.hut v1 v2` is definitionally equal to `G.tuh v2 v1`, but giving `hut` its own `def` preserves the LN's distinction between $\tuh$ and $\hut$ as separate syntactic shapes (relevant when `def_3_3` classifies edges by reading direction). A composition spelling such as `tuh ∘ Prod.swap` was rejected to keep `simp only [CDMG.hut]` land directly on the `Finset` membership without intermediate `Function.comp` / `Prod.swap_apply` rewrites.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `hut` = head-tail: arrow from `v2` to `v1`; LN macro $\hut$ (mirror of `tuh`).
-- Defined directly on `G.E` rather than as `tuh v2 v1` to keep `simp` unfolding flat.
def hut (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := (v2, v1) ∈ G.E

lean_blocks.code: def huh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := s(v1, v2) ∈ G.L
lean_blocks.explanation: Item 4 of the notation block: the primitive bidirected-edge relation. The letter pattern `huh` encodes head-on-left, head-on-right — two arrowheads, no tail — matching the LN macro $\huh$. The body is `s(v1, v2) ∈ G.L`, where `s(_, _)` is Mathlib's notation for `Sym2.mk` (the canonical constructor of the unordered-pair quotient `Sym2 Node`). Since `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` stores unordered pairs, the body literally reads "the unordered pair `{v1, v2}` is recorded in `L`". Because `Sym2` identifies `(a, b)` with `(b, a)` by construction, `s(v1, v2) = s(v2, v1)` holds definitionally, so the graph-theoretic symmetry $v_1 \huh v_2 \iff v_2 \huh v_1$ collapses to `rfl` after `unfold CDMG.huh`. The ordered binary signature `(v1 v2 : Node)` is kept — not a single `(s : Sym2 Node)` argument — because every downstream consumer (`def_3_3` adjacency, `def_3_5`'s `Sib`, `def_3_4`'s `.bidir` walk constructor) reaches `huh` from a context where the endpoints are already named separately; the `Sym2.mk` lives inside the body.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `huh` = head-head: bidirected edge between `v1` and `v2`; LN macro $\huh$.
-- `s(v1, v2)` is Mathlib's `Sym2.mk` notation; `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)`.
-- Symmetry `huh v1 v2 ↔ huh v2 v1` is `rfl` under the `Sym2` swap quotient.
def huh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := s(v1, v2) ∈ G.L

lean_blocks.code:
def suh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
lean_blocks.explanation: Item 5: the LN's star-head $\suh$ relation, where the leading $\star$ means "arrowhead or tail". Applied to $v_1 \suh v_2$, the head is fixed at `v2` and the left endpoint may carry either a tail (giving $\tuh$) or a head (giving $\huh$), so `suh` is the literal two-disjunct `G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2`. The disjunction is preserved as a named `def` rather than expanded via `abbrev`, so `unfold CDMG.suh` is opt-in at proof sites: downstream `rcases h with h | h` then splits into exactly the two LN cases. A merged form `(v1, v2) ∈ G.E ∪ G.L` was rejected because `E` and `L` need not be disjoint as subsets of `Node × Node`, which would erase the two-case decomposition. Note `suh` is *not* symmetric in general; the matched-pair identity is `G.suh v1 v2 ↔ G.hus v2 v1`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `suh` = star-head: left endpoint is tail-or-head, right endpoint is head.
-- Star = "arrowhead or tail" placeholder, expanding to exactly two cases on the left.
def suh (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  -- Literal disjunction mirroring LN; kept as `def` so `unfold` is opt-in downstream.
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

lean_blocks.code:
def hus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
lean_blocks.explanation: Item 6: the mirror of `suh`. The letter code `hus` is head-on-left, star-on-right, so the head is fixed at `v1` and the right endpoint may be either a tail (giving $\hut$: backwards directed) or a head (giving $\huh$: bidirected). The body is `G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2`, matching the LN's item 6 verbatim. Like `suh`, this is the "into-$v_1$" relation used downstream by `def_3_3` to classify edges pointing at a fixed vertex, and by `def_3_4` for collider-walk endpoint constraints. The symmetry corollary `G.hus v1 v2 ↔ G.suh v2 v1` follows by swapping the `hut`/`tuh` disjunct and applying `huh` symmetry; it is intentionally left as a one-line lemma rather than baked into the def.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `hus` = head-star: left endpoint is head, right endpoint is tail-or-head.
-- LN's "into-`v_1`" relation; matched pair of `suh` under argument swap.
def hus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  -- `hut` is the directed-into-`v_1` case; `huh` is the bidirected case.
  G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

lean_blocks.code:
def sus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2
lean_blocks.explanation: Item 7: the star-star $\sus$ adjacency relation. Both endpoints are stars, so naively four cases arise (tail-tail, tail-head, head-tail, head-head), but CDMGs admit no tail-tail edge type: directed edges in `E` carry exactly one tail and one head, and bidirected edges in `L` carry two heads. The missing $\tut$ case is therefore *excluded by definition*, and the `def` is the three-disjunct `G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2` — case analysis via `rcases` may treat these three as exhaustive (the operator clarification `[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail]` is the load-bearing justification). The disjunction order matches the LN reading. `sus` is graph-theoretically symmetric: swapping arguments exchanges the `tuh` and `hut` disjuncts and preserves `huh` by `hL_symm`, making `sus` the natural adjacency relation used in `def_3_3`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `sus` = star-star: both endpoints are tail-or-head; LN macro $\sus$.
-- Adjacency relation: "some edge type exists between `v1` and `v2`".
def sus (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop :=
  -- Three disjuncts only: CDMGs have no tail-tail edge, so `tut` is omitted by definition.
  -- Order matches LN item 7 verbatim so `rcases` case names align with LN reading.
  G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2

addition_to_the_LN:
[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail] CDMGs do not admit a tail-tail edge type; consequently the placeholder rule "the star stands for arrowhead or tail" yields only the three enumerated configurations in item 7 (the tail-tail case "$v_1 \tut v_2$" is excluded by definition, not omitted by oversight). Any case analysis on "$v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$" may assume exhaustiveness over exactly the three listed alternatives $\{\tuh, \hut, \huh\}$.

[huh_visual_symmetry_vs_ordered_pair_in_L] The relation $L$ is required to be symmetric: for all $v_1, v_2$, $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ if and only if $(v_2, v_1) \in L$. Consequently, $v_1 \huh v_2 \in G \iff v_2 \huh v_1 \in G$, and the symmetry of the $\huh$-disjunct propagates to items 5–7 accordingly.
design_choices:
**Encode `v ∈ G` as a `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance, not a `def CDMG.Mem` or a `Coe` to a vertex `Finset`.** The LN writes `v ∈ G` verbatim at every downstream use site (`def_3_5`'s `Pa^G(v)`, walk-endpoint constraints in `def_3_4`, the `v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G` phrasings of items 2-7). A `def` forces `G.Mem v` at call sites; a `Coe` to `Finset Node` shadows `Finset.instMembership` and type-erases vertex membership into opaque-`Finset` membership. Typeclass resolution disambiguates the outer `v ∈ G` from the body's inner `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` because the second type argument differs.

**`Node` and `[DecidableEq Node]` are introduced via a `variable` block.** Every following declaration shares the carrier and needs `DecidableEq Node` for `Finset` membership to compute; one `variable` line auto-binds the parameters into the declarations that use them, and `Type*` keeps `Node` universe-polymorphic.

**Items 2-7 are plain `def`s, not `notation` macros, `abbrev`s, or typeclass instances.** Dot-notation `G.tuh v1 v2` reads left-to-right like `$v_1 \tuh v_2 \in G$`. A `notation` macro would bake `G` in or lose graph context; an `abbrev` would auto-unfold and erase the named abstractions (`\suh`, `\hus`, `\sus` reappear by name in `def_3_3` and `def_3_4`); a typeclass has no synthesis target for a binary `Prop` predicate over `(G, v1, v2)`. Plain `def` keeps rewriting predictable — consumers `unfold` on demand.

**`hut` is `(v2, v1) ∈ G.E` directly, not `G.tuh v2 v1`, `tuh ∘ Prod.swap`, or via a stored `E_rev` field.** The LN's `def_3_3` treats `\hut` as a syntactic shape, not an iff-rewrite of `\tuh`. The direct body keeps `simp only [CDMG.hut]` landing on literal `Finset` membership — no `Function.comp_apply` / `Prod.swap_apply` to peel, no `E_rev` coherence lemma, no `Finset.mem_image` existential.

**Bidirected edges live in `Finset (Sym2 Node)`; `huh` reads `s(v1, v2) ∈ G.L`.** `Sym2 Node` is Mathlib's quotient of `Node × Node` by `(a, b) ∼ (b, a)`, so `Finset (Sym2 Node)` is literally the LN's `(V × V) / ((v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1))` (addition `[l_quotient_vs_ordered_pair_typing_inconsistent]` in `def_3_1`). Under the quotient `s(v1, v2) = s(v2, v1)` by `rfl`, so `G.huh v1 v2 ↔ G.huh v2 v1` is a one-line corollary with no symmetry axiom to chain — walk reversal in `def_3_4` and σ-separation symmetry in `claim_3_22` become orientation-free. The signature stays binary `(v1 v2 : Node)` because every consumer arrives with endpoints already named; the `Sym2.mk` lives inside the body.

**Items 5-7 are literal `Or`-disjunctions of items 2-4, not a merged `G.E ∪ G.L` and not `Bool`-valued.** Mirroring the LN enumeration reduces case analysis to `obtain h | h := h`. The merged `E ∪ L` spelling is ill-typed: `G.E : Finset (Node × Node)` and `G.L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` live over different carriers. `sus` is the three-disjunct `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh` in LN order; addition `[sus_omits_tail_tail_despite_star_eq_head_or_tail]` records that the placeholder rule "`\star` is arrowhead or tail" suggests a fourth `\tut` case but CDMGs admit no tail-tail edge type, so the missing case is excluded by definition and downstream `rcases` may treat the three disjuncts as exhaustive.

**Six separate `Prop` predicates, not a single `inductive EdgeType` plus a `G.edgeOf` lookup.** An ADT would force `match | EdgeType.tailHead => ...` at every use site — text the LN never writes. The set-builder forms of `def_3_5` (`Pa^G`, `Ch^G`, `Sib^G`), the walk-edge predicates of `def_3_4`, and the acyclicity chains of `def_3_6` all pattern-match against individual symbols; mirroring the LN one-to-one is paid once here.
